vitals.date: 2026: June 27 (planned route; 2026 race-day route was shortened to 124km/2,850m due to extreme heat — see eligibility notes)
vitals.field_size: 5,300 registration ceiling, not a confirmed starter count (30th edition, 100,000+ cumulative starters over its history)

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 79. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 74.

course_description.character: The planned flagship route runs from Tarascon-sur-Ariège to Auzat through the wooded, often narrow Ariège Pyrenees, climbing the Col du Portel via Péguère, Col de la Core, Col de Latrape, Col d'Agnes, and Port de Lers — a course defined by cumulative climbing and repeated valley-to-pass transitions rather than extreme altitude. Weather can materially rewrite the event: in 2026, extreme heat forced the flagship onto the shorter 124 km Mountagnole course, dropping Core and Latrape and leaving the Portel/Péguère and Agnes-Lers sequences as the principal tests. A separate non-competitive XXL ultra (298 km/7,500 m, run over two days) exists alongside the main timed field and should not be conflated with it.
course_description.signature_challenge: Col d'Agnes — 10 km at 8.2% average, a sustained first-category-grade effort that survives even when the flagship route is shortened.
suffering_zones: Col du Portel via Péguère — almost 32 km of continuous net ascent from Foix, long rather than uniformly steep, with Péguère providing the sting
Col d'Agnes — 10 km at 8.2% average, a sustained first-category effort
Agnes-Port de Lers sequence — only a short recovery before the Port de Lers counter-climb and descent toward Vicdessos
Plateau de Beille (XXL only) — approximately 15.8 km at 7.9%, one of the ultra's historical marquee finishing climbs

logistics.lodging_strategy: Book early in Tarascon-sur-Ariège; campsites, hotels, and guesthouses in prehistoric valley area.

history.origin_story: Thirty editions deep as of 2026, with over 100,000 cumulative starters across its history — real institutional depth for a regional Pyrenean cyclosportive.

biased_opinion.verdict: A Real Pyrenean Institution, Vulnerable to Its Own Numbers
biased_opinion.summary: Thirty editions and 100,000+ cumulative starters give L'Ariégeoise genuine institutional depth in the French Pyrenees, with a large registration ceiling and route choice spanning the 298 km non-competitive XXL down to a 74 km entry option. But 2026 exposed how much the event bends to conditions: extreme heat forced the flagship off its planned 182 km/4,150 m course and onto the 124 km Mountagnole route, dropping the Core and Latrape climbs entirely. Official data hygiene doesn't help — the same regulations document lists conflicting distance figures for the flagship route, and there's no verified, consolidated 2026 starter count to confirm the oft-repeated '5th largest gran fondo in France' claim, which reads as promotional language rather than a sourced ranking.
strengths: Thirty editions and 100,000+ cumulative starters — real institutional depth, not a new event borrowing prestige
A large registration ceiling (5,300) and extensive route choice from a 74 km entry option up to the 298 km non-competitive XXL ultra
Authentic, less commercial-feeling Pyrenean mountain roads with repeated major passes rather than a single showcase climb
Strong, well-spaced climbing on the core route — Col du Portel/Péguère and Col d'Agnes both deliver genuine Pyrenean difficulty
weaknesses: Weather can materially rewrite the event — in 2026 the flagship lost 58 km when heat forced a shift to the shorter Mountagnole route
Official route data is internally inconsistent, with different figures for the flagship distance appearing across the organizer's own pages
"France's 5th-largest gran fondo" is unverifiable — no current independent national participation table supports an exact ranking, and no consolidated 2026 starter total was published
Roads remain open to traffic; riders must obey the highway code, adding real risk on descents and in the bunch
The XXL is a non-competitive, semi-autonomous ultra, not a conventional timed gran fondo — it shouldn't be conflated with the main mass-start field
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A genuinely deep-rooted Pyrenean cyclosportive with real climbing and real scale — just don't assume the advertised course or field-size superlatives will hold on any given year.

final_verdict.one_liner: Pyrenean climbing showcase, France's 5th largest gran fondo.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes for genuine Pyrenean climbing with real institutional history. Check the current-year route before you commit — the flagship distance can and does change with conditions.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a similarly deep-rooted Pyrenean cyclosportive with a more stable advertised course, compare against other long-running French Pyrenees events.

videos.title: L'Ariégeoise 2017
videos.description:
Le parcours XXL, 170 km (D+ 4100 m), au départ de Tarascon-Sur-Ariège, via:

- Col de Port
- Col de la Core
- Col de Saraillé
- Port de Lers
- Montée de Goulier-Neige
videos.curation_reason: Best view count with detailed course description showing the challenging XXL route with 4100m elevation gain through major Pyrenean cols.

videos.title: L'Ariégeoise Cyclosportive 2022 - le Parcours - Cols dans les Pyrénées
videos.description:
L'Ariégeoise est une épreuve cyclosportive qui se déroule au départ de Tarascon sur ariège et qui arrive aux cabanes. 

Les cyclistes pourront monter les cols de Montségur (1059m) puis celui de la Croix des morts (898m), mais également le Port de Pailhères et le col du Chioula.

Retrouvez toutes les informations sur le site de la course : https://www.cyclosport-ariegeoise.com/ariegeoise-fr/

En savoir plus sur Le port de Pailhère : https://www.cols-cyclisme.com/pyrenees-est/france/port-de-pailheres-depuis-mijanes-c323.htm
et sur Le col du Chioula : https://www.cols-cyclisme.com/pyrenees-est/france/col-du-chioula-depuis-ax-les-thermes-c237.htm
Tout savoir sur Montségur : http://www.montsegur.fr/

*******************************

suivez-nous sur : http://www.izirando.com

eligibility.notes: 30th edition completed 27 June 2026, but extreme heat forced the flagship route from the planned 182km/4,150m onto the shorter 124km Mountagnole course on race day. Field size in vitals is the registration ceiling, not a verified starter count — no consolidated 2026 total was published.
